text:
Olympics

Quincy Hall won Olympic gold in the men’s 400m final on Wednesday by just four-hundredths of a second in another dramatic photo finish at Paris 2024.

Britain’s Matthew Hudson-Smith set a new European record but he was pipped at the post by Hall, who won gold in a personal best 43.40.

Elsewhere, Noah Lyles — who triumphed in the men’s 100m final on Sunday night in an even closer final — progressed in the 200m by finishing second in his semifinal.

At Le Golf National, defending Olympic champion and world No 1 Nelly Korda carded a level-par 72 in her first round. And the United States women’s basketball team beatNigeria88-74.

Medal table|How to follow|U.S. women's basketball

For the first time in Olympic beach volleyball history, the United States will not medal in the event.

Qatar’sCherif YounousseandAhmed Tijaneliminated the final remaining U.S. duo —Andy BeneshandMiles Partain— in a 2-0 sweep in the men’s quarterfinals.

On Monday, Norway’sAnders MolandChristian Sørumknocked out the other U.S. men’s pair ofChase Budinger(pictured above) andMiles Evansin the round of 16.

On the women’s side, Canada’sMelissa Humana-ParedesandBrandie Wilkersontook down U.S. pairKristen NussandTaryn Klothin the round of 16, while Switzerland’sTanja HüberliandNina Brunneradvanced past AmericansSara HughesandKelly Cheng— the defending world champions — in a quarterfinal match yesterday.

Those Swiss and Canadian teams will face each other in the first semifinal tomorrow.

With the 2028 Olympics in Los Angeles, a hub for the sport, perhaps the American teams will fare better on their home turf.

TheUnited Stateswomen’s basketball team is into the semifinals of the Paris 2024 tournament after a comfortable 88-74 victory overNigeria.

The U.S. will next playAustraliafor a place in Sunday’s final, against either host nationFranceorBelgium.

GO FURTHER

Team USA routs Nigeria, advances to semifinal vs. Australia

One week ago,Braziliansoccer legendMarta’s Olympics — her sixth, and final major tournament — looked to be over. She had come in with her boot high on a rash challenge in Brazil’s final group stage match against Spain.

A deserved red card was thrust into the air, and Marta could not stop the tears that came, spilling them into the grass at Stade de Bordeaux.

Down to 10 players, Brazil gave up two goals in that final group stage match. They snuck through into the knockout rounds as a third-place team, thanks to the slimmest of margins: a -2 goal differential, compared to Australia’s -3.

For the next two matches, all she could do was wait and watch. Now Marta, twenty years out from making her Olympic debut, has been gifted a new chance to write her own ending here in France in the gold medal match. Perfect endings are hard. They sometimes feel impossible in this sport.

But Marta has been writing her own story, full of hunger, full of passion, and most of all, full of joy, for a while now. No matter what, she’s heading back to Orlando with a medal around her neck.Read more below.

GO FURTHER

Marta’s legacy is defiance, hunger and joy. But how will it end at the Olympics?

There was plenty of drama earlier today whenIndia’s Vinesh Phogatmissed weight to lose her place in today’s women’s 50kg freestyle final againstSarah Hildebrandt.

That promotedCuba’s Yusneylis Guzman— but Hildebrant did not let the late change of opponent flummox her as she recorded a 3-0 points victory in this evening’s final.

Bronzes went toChina’s Feng ZiqiandJapan’s Susaki Yui.

Ethiopianstar runnerLamecha Girmaexited the track on a stretcher after taking a hard fall while attempting to clear a barrier on the backside of the final lap in Wednesday’s 3000m men’s steeplechase.

The 23-year-old was accelerating down the back straightaway as he leaped to clear the hurdle while competitors were on his right and left. Girma appeared to clip the barrier with his trailing right knee and fell directly onto his upper body.

From there, he was seen rolling onto his stomach as the race finished.

On the broadcast, Peacock play-by-play commentator Leigh Diffey noted minutes later that Girma remained motionless. Girma, who holds the world record in the event and won silver in Tokyo, was later seen with a neck brace on as he was carried off the track on a backboard by four medical personnel.

“In 40 years of commentating running and the steeplechase, I have never seen a fall that heavy,” Diffey said solemnly as he signed off.

GO FURTHER

Ethiopia’s Lamecha Girma stretchered off track after falling during steeplechase final

International Olympic Committee officials have repeatedly said thatLin Yu-tingand another boxer fighting for gold,Imane Khelif, have met all requirements to compete at the Paris Games in women’s divisions. Their official documents show they were assigned female at birth and that they identify as women, and they have competed as such for years.

Details of the testing that led the IBA to strip medals from Lin and Khelif last year have not been thoroughly detailed. And the IBA’s attempt at clarity earlier this week devolved into a chaotic scene of imprecise accusations, contradictory descriptions about gender and sex and wider complaints about years of spats between the IBA and the IOC.

“We desperately need a new federation to run boxing,” IOC spokesman Mark Adams said Tuesday.

For a gold medal, Lin will faceJulia Szeremetaof Poland, who pulled off a remarkable turnaround in her semifinal againstNesthy Petecioof the Philippines after falling unanimously behind in the first round.

Petecio andEsra Yildiz Kahramanwill each receive bronze medals since there is no third-place bout in Olympic boxing.

Lin Yu-tingofTaiwanclinched a spot in the gold medal fight for her featherweight division much in the same way that she has handled the noisy gender controversy that has defined this Olympic boxing tournament: by staying on the outside, weathering some flurries and sticking to a quiet yet technically sound strategy.

Esra Yildiz KahramanofTurkeytook an aggressive approach toward Lin in their semifinal in the 57-kilogram, 125-pound division, moving inside immediately to try to force a brawl. It was a reasonable thought – Kahraman is 4 inches shorter than Lin and would have been picked apart had she remained on the perimeter. But Lin is a comfortable counterpuncher and delivered too much punishment as  Kahraman ventured closer. Lin took all three rounds on all five of the judges’ scorecards, and skipped happily in the final seconds as she celebrated her coming gold medal fight on Saturday night.

Until the very end, the bout had few signs of the discord that has followed Lin’s fights. Kahraman touched gloves with Lin beforehand and congratulated her afterward, but as she exited the ring she made an X symbol with her index fingers, an apparent reference to uncertainty about Lin’s disqualification from the world championship in 2023 because of a gender test administered by the International Boxing Association.

Not that long afterPanipak Wongpattanakitdefended her taekwondo crown, another reigning Olympic champion has emerged victorious in Paris.

Hou ZhihuiofChinahas won the women’s 49kg gold by setting a new Olympic record.

She lifted 89kg in the snatch and 117kg in the clean and jerk for a total of 206kg, to beatRomania’s Mihaela Cambeiby 1kg.

And there’s more success forThailandthis evening, withSurodchana Khambaowinning the bronze.

Jesse M:“What a gutsy comeback in the final fifty! Quincy looked cooked halfway through the turn, but just willed his way through. Incredible running!”

Martin R:“Twice in as many days that Team USA pip Team GB to gold! 1500m and now 400m. It doesn't sting at all... Kerr and Hudson-Smith both ran PBs, but both Hocker and Hall ran tremendously, well done to all!”

John K:“Another amazing final race with an American showing the heart and grit for Gold!”

TheUnited Statesare in control againstNigeriaas they aim for a place in the final four of the women’s basketball tournament.

You can follow play-by-play updates of that game with my colleagueMichael Dominskiand the team by clickinghere.

What an utterly enthralling race. But there are some concerning scenes out on the track.

Morocco’sSoufiane El Bakkaliwins the men’s 3000m steeplechase final. He defends his Olympic title with a time of 8:06.05.

Kenneth Rooksof theUnited Statescomes second,Kenya’s Abraham Kibiwotis third.

It was an incredible race:Mohamed Tindouftof Morocco was briefly in front before Rooks went on the attack and appeared to seize control.

El Bakkali was lurking throughout, as was the world record holderLamecha Girma. But Girma fell and Rooks tired down the straight, allowing the champion El Bakkali to storm through and win gold again.

But there is concern for Girma who fell very, very heavily. He receives medical treatment for some minutes before departing the track on a stretcher. Hopefully he is okay.

The first taekwondo medal of the Paris 2024 Games has just been awarded and it has been won by a familiar face.

Thailand’sPanipak Wongpattanakithas defended her women’s 49kg title by beatingChina’sGuo Qingin a close final.

The two bronze medals go toIran’s Mobina NematzadehandCroatia’s Lena Stojkovic.

Nina Kennedyhas won pole vault gold forAustraliaafter clearing 4.90m.Katie Moonof theUnited Statesattempted 4.95m with her final jump but the reigning champion brought the bar down as Kennedy claimed victory.

Canada’s Alysha Newmanwins bronze.

Upset alert!

Lithuania’sMykolas Aleknamust have thought he was on course for gold when he set a new Olympic record in the men’s discus throw earlier today.

But, in a sensational turn of events, his new record lasted only a matter of minutes before being bettered by Jamaica’sRoje Stonawith an epic throw of 70.00m!

The favourites failed to deliver with 2022 world championKristjan Cehfinishing fourth and defending Olympic championDaniel Stahldown in seventh.

Alekna had to make do with silver and a short-lived record with Australia’sMatthew Dennywinning bronze.

OhMatt Hudson-Smith! So close! The line just would not come for the Briton in the 400m as lowered his own European record but was pipped to Olympic gold by AmericanQuincy Hall.

Forgive me a moment of indulgence but I remember watching Hudson-Smith aged 19 when he took nearly a second (0.83secs) off his personal best in only his sixth 400m race in Glasgow in 2014. He could barely speak after that; so enormous was what he achieved. And a decade on, he's run 43.44secs, fifth-quickest in history, to win Olympic silver.

AmericanQuincy Halllooked to be fading coming around the final turn in the men's 400m final, with a face showing serious signs of pain, but he dug deep in the final 100 meters to overtake several runners and win the gold medal by four hundredths of a second!

Matthew Hudson-Smith(Britain) takes second, three tenths ahead ofMuzalaSamukonga(Zambia) in third.Jereem Richards(Trinidad and Tobago) andKirani James(Grenada) are kept off the podium in fourth and fifth.

This should be a great one!

The big names to watch here areKirani James(Grenada),Matthew Hudson-Smith(Britain) andQuincy Hall(United States).

Here are the eight women who have qualified for Friday's 400m final.

AmericanKendall Ellisjust misses out on qualifying by 0.23 seconds, finishing in ninth place.

The women's pole vault final is down to three competitors, AmericanKatie Moon, CanadianAlysha Newmanand AussieNina Kennedy.

They are currently attempting 4.90m, which was Moon's gold medal-winning height at Tokyo 2020.
title: Everything that happened on Day 12 of the 2024 Paris Olympics - The Athletic
author: The Athletic
description: Quincy Hall won Olympic gold in the men’s 400m final and the United States women’s basketball team beat Nigeria 88-74
